coredata.dc:title: Transparent flexible graphene quantum dot-(PVDF-HFP) piezoelectric nanogenerator
coredata.dc:creator: Badatya, Simadri
Kumar, Anil
Sharma, Charu
Srivastava, Avanish Kumar
Chaurasia, Jamana Prasad
Gupta, Manoj Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report highly sensitive graphene quantum dot (GQDs) reinforced poly (vinylidene fluoride)-co-hexafluoride propylene (PVDF-HFP) based flexible piezoelectric nanogenerator for the first time. Formation of β-phase in GQDs /PVDF-HFP composite film was confirmed using XRD diffraction and FT-IR spectroscopy techniques. Flexible transparent piezoelectric nanogenerator fabricated from the GQDs/PVDF-HFP composite film exhibits very high output voltage of 6 V and output current of 25 nA compared to signal generated from pristine PVDF-HFP nanogenerator. The drastic improvement in the PVDF-HFP may be attributed to the alignment of electric dipoles induced by the local electric field cause by conductive GQDs. Performance of GQDs/PVDF-HFP has also been real time demonstrated as self-powered human breathing and finger movement sensors which make it suitable for promising wearable energy harvester and for powering nanosystems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy storage and conversion
Nanocomposites
Piezoelectric materials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of GQDs and PVDF-HFP flexible nanogenerator

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis and fabrication process of flexible and transparent GQDs/PVDF-HFP nanogenerators, (b) X-ray diffraction of as grown pristine PVDF-HFP and GQDs reinforced PVDF-HFP sample, (c) FTIR spectrum of as grown pristine PVDF-HFP and GQDs reinforced PVDF-HFP samples.
(a) Low and (b) high magnified FE-SEM image of graphene quantum dots, (c, d) FE-SEM images of as grown GQDs reinforced PVDF-HFP composite film.

(a) Piezoelectric output voltage, (b) output current from GQDS/PVDF-HFP composite device under vertical compressive force (forward connection),(c) Piezoelectric output voltage response under hand/wrist movement and, (d) Output voltage under breathing condition.

head.ce:title: Transparent flexible graphene quantum dot-(PVDF-HFP) piezoelectric nanogenerator

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh 462026, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: CSIR-Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh 462026, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report highly sensitive graphene quantum dot (GQDs) reinforced poly (vinylidene fluoride)-co-hexafluoride propylene (PVDF-HFP) based flexible piezoelectric nanogenerator for the first time. Formation of β-phase in GQDs /PVDF-HFP composite film was confirmed using XRD diffraction and FT-IR spectroscopy techniques. Flexible transparent piezoelectric nanogenerator fabricated from the GQDs/PVDF-HFP composite film exhibits very high output voltage of 6 V and output current of 25 nA compared to signal generated from pristine PVDF-HFP nanogenerator. The drastic improvement in the PVDF-HFP may be attributed to the alignment of electric dipoles induced by the local electric field cause by conductive GQDs. Performance of GQDs/PVDF-HFP has also been real time demonstrated as self-powered human breathing and finger movement sensors which make it suitable for promising wearable energy harvester and for powering nanosystems.

ce:section.ce:para: Piezoelectric nanogenerators have recently attracted huge interest because of their ability to convert mechanical energy into electricity and their integration in self-powered nanosensors and wearable/portable electronic systems [1]. Recently, various nanogenerator based on ZnO nanowire, Zn2SiO4nanorods, PbZrTiO3thin film have reported [2,3]. However, due to sophisticated growth and fabrication methods of piezoelectric inorganic materials and their nanogenerators, the fabrication of transparent and flexible nanogenerators with high electric output are still challenging [4,5]. Moreover, organic poly vinylidene fluoride (PVDF) has been also used to fabricate the nanogenerator due to its light weight, good breakdown strength, process ability and formation of polar β-phase which creates piezoelectricity in PVDF. In addition, copolymers of PVDF such as poly(vinylidene fluoride-hexafluoro propylene) (PVDF-HFP) has also attracted tremendous interest due to their improved piezoelectric, electrical and mechanical properties and transparency. The piezoelectricity of PVDF-HFP is mainly contributed by polar crystalline phases such as β-phase and γ-phase, rather than α-phases. Among the various crystalline phases, the electrocactive β-phase imparts the highest dipole moment resulting in high piezoelectricity [6]. However, to achieve the β-phase PVDF-HFP, numerous methods such as doping with piezoelectric nanostructures, electrical poling and thermal treatments, stretching are employed [7]. Particularly, spin coating method and reinforcement with the nano fillers are the most effective method to produce the self-poled piezoelectric film by introducing specific polymer-filler interactions and stabilize the β-phase nano crystals [8]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted wonderful attention due to biocompatibility, excellent solubility, low energy minima, large surface area and good surface grafting through π-π conjugating bonding [9,10]. In present work, crystalline GQDs were synthesised and used as nanofiller to achieve the β-phase in PVDF-HFP. A highly sensitive flexible and transparent piezoelectric nanogenerator was fabricated for the first time using the GQDs/PVDF-HFP on the indium tin oxide (ITO) coated polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDs and PVDF-HFP flexible nanogenerator
ce:section.ce:para: Graphite powder (Sigma, 99.99%, ultra-pure) was used as a precursor to the synthesis of GQDs though chemical exfoliation method. The detailed synthesis process of GQDs is given in the Supporting Information. To prepare the thin film of the GQDs/PVDF-HFP, 10 ml N, N-dimethyleformamide (DMF) was mixed in 20 µl GQDs dispersed solution. This mixture was heated up to 100 °C on a magnetic stirrer until the evaporation of water. After that, a 30 ml hydrazine hydrate was added drop by drop for the reduction of graphene oxide quantum dots into reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs). Finally, 2 g of PVDF-HFP was added in the DMF (N, N-dimethylformamide) and resultant solution was stirrer until to get transparent solution. The resultant solution was spin coated onto ITO coated PET substrate under speed of 2000 rpm for 60 s duration. The optical image of GQDs solution fluoresced with an excitation wavelength of 254 nm from UV lamp as shown in top side of Fig. 1 a. The flexible piezoelectric nanogenerator was fabricated by deposition of the conduting ITO electrode on the top surface of the composite film. The schemetic process of fabrication of the device and the orginal photographic image of the flexible nanogenerator based on the PVDF-HFP-GQDs is shown in left and bottom side of Fig. 1a.

X-ray diffraction (Rigaku Mini Flex II) with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.540 A°) was used to investigate the crystal structure of samples. Functional groupspresented in the PVDF-HFP and GQDs were studied using Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (FEI Nova NanoSEM 230) was used to study the morphological properties of the samples. Piezoelectric output voltage from the nanogenerators was measured using Tektronix-MDO3052, USA and current was measured using Keithley electrometer (Model: 6517B, USA).

Crystal structure of pristine PVDF-HFP film and GQDs-PVDF-HFP composite film were investigated using the X-Ray Diffraction method (Fig. 1b). A large peak observed at 2θ = 20.7° corresponds to (100) and (200) confirmed the formation of crystalline β phase of PVDF-HFP. Small intensity peaks observed at 2θ = 18.2, 28.6, and 38.0° correspond to (100), (110) and (021) denotes α-phase of PVDF-HFP [11]. The XRD diffraction peak around 31.2° corresponds to (222) plane which is characteristic peak of the ITO [12]. A diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.2°(JCPDS No-41-1487) which is corresponds to is signature (002) plane of graphene quantum dot presented in the composite sample [13]. XRD analysis clearly indicates that the reinforcement of GQDs in PVDF-HFP film reduces the α-phase and drastically increases the amount of the β-phase in the composite film as compared to the pristine PVDF-HFP film.

Moreover, FTIR spectral investigation of pristine PVDF-HFP film and GQDs/PVDF-HFP films were carried out to studies the GQDs induced β phase in PVDF-HFP (Fig. 1c). The bands related to β-phase at 840 cm−1, 876 cm−1, 1071 cm−1, 1170 cm−1 and 1400 cm-1were observed in the samples [14]. The band detected at 745 cm-1 and 840 cm-1are attributed to the CH2 rocking vibration and the band occurs at1400 cm−1 and 3032 cm-1 are attributed to symmetric and antisymmetric stretching vibrations of CH2 [15]. The band appeared at 876 cm-1 and 1071 cm-1confirms mixed mode of combined CF2 and CC symmetric stretching vibrations and CCC, respectively [16]. The observed bands at1633 cm-1 and 3290 cm-1 are attributed to the bending of CO and OH stretching of GQDs [17]. It is worth to point out that an absorption characteristic of α-phase observed in pristine PVDF-HFP was absent in the GQDs reinforced PVDF-HFP composite film, suggesting the formation β-phase due to poling of internal electric field created by GQDs. Fig. 2 shows the surface morphologies of GQDs and PVDF-HFP composite reinforced with the GQDs. The morphology of the graphene quantum dots indicates the formation of well-known circular morphology with diameter in range of 10–70 nm (Fig. 2a, b). Surface morphology of the GQDs/PVDF-HFP film was also measured and obtained FE-SEM image is shown in the Fig. 2c, d. FE-SEM results indicate the distribution of GQDs in PVDF-HFP matrix and exhibits the chain/rod like structure of β- phase PVDF-HFP.

The piezoelectric performance of GQDs/PVDF-HFP composite film is measured under vertical compressive force of 1 kgf. Fig. 3 a, b shows the output voltage measured from GQDs/PVDF-HFP nanogenerator. The device exhibits very high output voltage of 6 V and current of 25 nA, respectively. The output voltage generated from GQDs/PVDF-HFP was very high compared to that of other reported nanogenerator based on PVDF nanostructures. To compare the results, a piezoelectric nanogenerator based on pristine PVDF-HFP film was also fabricated and their output voltage and current were measured under same condition. An output voltage of about 2.9 V and very minute current of 9 nA were measured under same force (Fig. S1). The remarkable enhanced power output from GQDs/PVDF-HFP nanogenerator may be attributed to in-situ poling and formation of piezoelectric β phase induced by the conductive GQDs in polymer matrix [18,19]. Moreover, increase in the output voltage/current is also related to the dramatic increment in the number of dipoles per unit area as compare to the bulk pristine PVDF-HFP. Switching-polarity tests of both the devices were also carried out and an electric signals in opposite direction are generated from nanogenerator which further confirmed that signal is generated from the device rather than instruments and others errors (Fig. S1). Real time demonstration of flexible GQDs/PVDF-HFP nanogenerator was carried out and device was integrated with hand and neck parts of the human body. The output voltage was measured under the bending/motion of hand (Fig. 3c) and under breathing condition (Fig. 3d). The piezoelectric output voltage with an average output voltage of 80 mV and 100 mV were obtained under hand movement and breathing condition. These results indicate the huge potential of the GQDs/PVDF-HFP not only for scavenging mechanical energy but also as power source for wearable electronics and powering small scale electronic devices.

In summary, we have synthesized and fabricated transparent flexible GQDs/PVDF-HFP nanogenerators for the first time. The structural and functional groups characterizations of the samples was investigated by XRD and FTIR studies which showed the formation of β-phase in GQDs/PVDF-HFP. The incorporation of GQDs enhances the nucleation and stabilization of the piezoelectric polar β-phase. Piezoelectric nanogenerator based on the GQDs/PVDF-HFP generated very output voltage of 6 V and current of 25 nA compared to output power generated from pristine PVDF-HFP composite film. The enhancement in the output voltage was discussed in the light of dipole alignment, formation of β-phase by locally induced electric filed cause by GQDs.

Simadri Badatya: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Anil Kumar: Investigation, Methodology. Charu Sharma: Data curation, Validation. Avanish Kumar Srivastava: Supervision. Jamana Prasad Chaurasia: Supervision. Manoj Kumar Gupta: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: S. Badatya is grateful to the University Grant Commission, Govt. of India, for awarding the SRF. M.K.G. is grateful to the Department of Science & Technology, Govt. of India, for awarding the DST-INSPIRE Faculty Fellowship [IFA-13 PH-81].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2021.129493.
